detailed_analysis: enumerator.py is the EnumeratorMixin half of the DiscoveryWalker use case (Layer 2). It is well-scoped to a single responsibility (per-cell on-disk artifact enumeration and registration into the pairs dict), stays under the 300-line module limit (274 lines), and every function is under the 40-line limit. It correctly upholds the Dependency Rule: it imports only inward (entities, ports, gateways) and references no concrete tool name; tool_name flows in as a parameter (goal 5 / NFR-11). Fault isolation (FR-02) is honoured: every read/parse failure is caught, logged via structured logging.exception/logging.error (NFR-13), and the affected pair is blocked rather than crashing the loop. The dominant code-quality issue is DRY: the pair_id extract -> None-mint -> validate -> block flow is duplicated almost verbatim between _add_keyed_map_slot_artifact (lines 98-131) and _add_agentic_tool_artifact (lines 171-206), differing only in the block helper and the digest function. Secondary issues are long parameter lists (up to 10 params) carrying a recurring (pairs, blocked_pair_ids, state) data clump, and a magic-number fallback mtime=0.0. No security smells, no hardcoded secrets, no global mutable state. No CRITICAL findings.

findings.problem: The pair_id extract / None-mint / validate / block-on-failure / construct-info / insert pipeline is implemented twice. Any change to the identity-handling or blocking policy (a real concern for I-1 and US-03 AC-8/AC-10) must be edited in two places and kept in lock-step (Shotgun Surgery risk). The two paths already diverge subtly: the per-file path logs the read failure separately (L171-179) while the slot path has no read step, and validation failure logging strings differ ('slot=%s' vs path only), which is exactly the drift DRY exists to prevent.

findings.evidence: def _add_keyed_map_slot_artifact(self, tool_name, customization_type, io, shared_path, slot_key, slot_text, mtime, pairs, blocked_pair_ids, state) -> None:
findings.problem: 10 parameters (excluding self) far exceeds the >5 threshold. _discover_shared_keyed_map (7), _add_agentic_tool_artifact (7), and _insert_agentic_tool (6) also exceed it. The signatures thread the whole enumeration context positionally, which is error-prone (argument-order mistakes) and signals a missing parameter object for the per-poll accumulator triple.

findings.evidence: The triple (pairs: dict[str, CustomizationArtifactInfo], blocked_pair_ids: set[str], state: dict[str, CustomizationArtifactState]) co-occurs in _discover_shared_keyed_map (L48-50), _add_keyed_map_slot_artifact (L93-95), _add_agentic_tool_artifact (L166-168); the pair (pairs, blocked_pair_ids) recurs in _insert_agentic_tool (L213-215); (state, blocked_pair_ids) recurs in the three _block_state_owner* helpers (L243-244, L253-255, L264-265).
findings.problem: The same group of accumulator parameters travels together through every method. This is the canonical Data Clump smell: it should be a single per-poll DiscoveryAccumulator object (pairs + blocked_pair_ids + state), which would also collapse the long parameter lists flagged in CQ-02.

0.0 is an unnamed sentinel for 'mtime unavailable'. Its intent (epoch-zero / oldest-possible timestamp, which feeds conflict resolution by mtime per US-03 AC-3 / US-06) is not self-evident. A named constant (e.g. MISSING_MTIME = 0.0) would document that a failed stat deliberately makes the slot the conflict loser. Compare with L203 where path.stat().st_mtime is used with no fallback at all, an inconsistency between the two paths.

findings.variant: Broad except masking a no-raise contract
findings.problem: Per the port contract (architecture S5.1 rule 3 / US-10), extract_pair_id MUST NOT raise on malformed input - it returns None. Catching Exception around it is defensive belt-and-braces that is not silent (it logs structured context and blocks the pair, satisfying FR-02/NFR-13), so it is NOT a true C3 silent-swallow. It is flagged INFO only because a broad except around a documented no-raise boundary hides any genuine contract violation behind a generic 'cannot extract pair_id' message rather than surfacing it as the distinct bug it would be. Acceptable as fault isolation, noted for completeness.

spec_adherence.notes: No spec or architecture deviations found. The file upholds the Dependency Rule (Layer-2 use case importing only inward), references no concrete tool name (goal 5 / NFR-11 / architecture S1), isolates faults per pair (FR-02), logs structured failures naming tool/type/path (NFR-13), validates pair_id at the boundary (I-1, fail-fast S8), and is on the read/discovery path only so the destructive-write archive invariant (I-3 / NFR-01) and the secret_policy egress boundary (NFR-15, goal 6) do not apply here. US-10 AC-3 kind-restricted participation is enforced upstream in walker.py, not in this file.

verdict_summary: Clean, well-layered discovery mixin; only structural debt is DRY duplication of the pair_id pipeline plus long-parameter-list / data-clump noise. No CRITICAL findings; spec-conformant.
